title: Practice Exam 1
description: Comprehensive practice exam covering all 8 lectures: NPD process, concept screening and testing, conjoint analysis, market experiments, project management, brand management, and brand sustainability.

questions.question: What is the research-supported failure rate for new products, according to the evidence presented in this course?

options.text: 10–20% of new products fail
Approximately 40% of new products fail
70–80% of new products fail
Over 90% of new products fail

questions.explanation: Research evidence (Markham and Lee, Journal of Product Innovation Management, 2013) places the failure rate at approximately 40%. The popular press figure of 80–90% is not supported by careful empirical studies. The top 25% of firms achieve an 82.2% success rate, meaning even the best firms still see substantial failure — this is why a structured NPD process is essential.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct AMA (American Marketing Association) definition of a brand?

options.text: The perceived quality of a product as assessed by consumers in a target market
A promise made by a company to its customers about product performance
A name, term, sign, symbol, or design, or a combination thereof, intended to identify and differentiate a seller's offerings from competitors
The financial premium value that a well-known product commands over a generic alternative

questions.explanation: The AMA definition precisely identifies the functional, identifying role of brands: differentiating one seller's offerings from competitors through names, symbols, and designs. Options (a) and (d) describe brand equity consequences, not the brand itself. Option (b) describes brand promise, a downstream concept. The distinction between a product (what the company makes) and a brand (what consumers perceive) is foundational to the course.

questions.question: Name the three conditions that must be satisfied simultaneously in the Three Innovation Challenge (Venn diagram) framework, and give an example of a product that satisfies all three.
questions.correctAnswer: The three conditions are: (1) Desirable to People, (2) Technically Feasible, and (3) Business Viable. The iRobot Roomba satisfies all three: consumers wanted automated vacuuming (desirable), the celestial guidance technology was achievable (feasible), and the product category proved commercially sustainable at consumer price points (viable).

questions.question: According to the Terwiesch and Ulrich survey of 524 managers, what percentage of new product opportunities are internally generated (Push innovation)?
questions.explanation: The Terwiesch and Ulrich survey found that 46% of opportunities are internally generated. The remaining sources include customers (23%), competitive products (11%), sales force (5%), and others (universities, independent inventors, distributors, suppliers). This highlights that while customer-sourced opportunities are important, internal capability-driven innovation is actually the most common origin of new product ideas.

questions.question: The RWW (Real-Win-Worth It) Analysis asks three sequential questions about a product opportunity. Which question maps to 'Business Viable' in the Three Innovation Challenge?

options.text: Is it Real? — Does a genuine market need exist?
Can we Win? — Can the firm establish sustainable competitive advantage?
Is it Worth It? — Will the investment generate sufficient financial returns at acceptable risk?
Is it Feasible? — Can the technology be developed within budget?

questions.explanation: 'Is it Worth It?' directly maps to Business Viability in the innovation challenge. 'Is it Real?' maps to People Desirability, and 'Can we Win?' maps to Technical Feasibility. Option (d) is not one of the three RWW questions. The RWW framework operationalizes the Desirability/Viability/Feasibility triangle into three practical go/no-go questions used at the end of Phase 1.

questions.question: In the Nike yoga apparel in Singapore market sizing exercise, the TAM was SGD 300 million. If 60% of consumers buy premium-branded products and Nike captures 10% of that segment, what is the Serviceable Obtainable Market (SOM)?

options.text: SGD 18 million
SGD 30 million
SGD 180 million
SGD 300 million

questions.explanation: SAM = TAM × 60% = 300M × 0.60 = SGD 180 million. SOM = SAM × 10% = 180M × 0.10 = SGD 18 million. This is the number that matters for financial planning — not the full TAM. Option (c) is the SAM; option (d) is the TAM; option (b) confuses the calculation by applying 10% directly to TAM.

questions.question: What type of customer need did P&G's Swiffer address, and why was ethnographic research necessary to discover it?
questions.correctAnswer: Swiffer addressed a latent need. Latent needs are ones customers cannot consciously articulate. Design Continuum researchers observed that consumers' floors were already clean before they arrived, revealing an emotional/identity dimension — 'our floors are a reflection of ourselves.' They also noticed brooms were hard to clean. Neither insight would emerge from a survey or focus group because customers could not describe these needs without being observed in their natural environment. Ethnography (observational research in the home) was necessary precisely because latent needs exist below conscious articulation.

questions.question: In the Concept Screening Matrix (Pugh Matrix), what scale is used to evaluate each concept relative to the reference concept?

options.text: A 1–5 numerical scale where 3 equals the reference
+, 0, – (better than, same as, or worse than the reference)
A 1–10 satisfaction scale
A percentage score out of 100%

questions.explanation: The screening matrix uses a simple three-symbol scale: + (better than reference), 0 (same as reference), – (worse than reference). The net score = Plusses – Minuses. The 1–5 numerical scale in option (a) belongs to the Concept Scoring Matrix, which is a different, later-stage tool. Confusing these two scales is one of the most common mistakes in concept evaluation.

questions.question: In the insulin syringe case study, which concept won the final Concept Scoring Matrix and why is this result particularly instructive?

options.text: Concept A (Master Cylinder) — because it ranked first in screening and maintained its lead in scoring
Concept DF (Lever Stop) — a combination of Concepts D and F, which had individually been eliminated in the screening stage
Concept E (Swash Ring) — because it achieved a perfect 5 on Dose Metering Accuracy, the highest-weighted criterion
Concept G+ (Enhanced Dial Screw) — because it performed best on Ease of Manufacture (weight 20%)

questions.explanation: Concept DF won with a total weighted score of 3.45. The instructive insight is that DF was a combination of Concepts D and F — both of which had been eliminated in the earlier screening stage. This demonstrates that even eliminated concepts may have features worth salvaging through combination. Concept A ranked first in screening but was not the winner in scoring. The scoring matrix is not just a selection tool — it encourages concept refinement and combination.

questions.question: According to the Product Specification formula, which three components must all be present for a specification to be complete and actionable?

options.text: Feature, function, and form
Need, benefit, and technology
Metric, value (or range), and units
Criteria, weight, and rating

questions.explanation: A product specification requires Metric + Value + Units. Example: 'Total Mass | 8.5–10 | kg.' A metric without a value is not actionable; a value without units is ambiguous. Option (a) describes concept generation inputs; option (b) describes the need-form-technology model; option (d) describes concept scoring matrix components.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the 'best average product' trap in concept scoring?

options.text: Selecting the concept with the highest weighted score on the single most important criterion
Selecting a concept that scores moderately on all criteria, potentially missing a concept that excels on the highest-weight criteria even if weaker overall
Averaging all concepts' total scores to find the mean performance across the team
Choosing the reference concept as a safe fallback when all new concepts score below 3.0

questions.explanation: The 'best average product' trap occurs when a concept scores adequately across all criteria, earning a high total score, while a different concept excels specifically on the most critical criteria (highest weights) but is weaker elsewhere. The latter may actually be the better design choice. The solution is a sensitivity check: examine which criteria are driving the ranking and whether eliminating weaknesses through combination would produce something superior. The scoring matrix is a thinking tool, not an automatic decision machine.

questions.question: At which stage of the product development lifecycle does qualitative concept testing occur?

options.text: Detail Design phase
Testing and Refinement phase
Concept Development phase
Production Ramp-Up phase

questions.explanation: Qualitative concept testing occurs during the Concept Development phase — it answers 'Should we build this?' before any prototype exists. Alpha testing (internal) and Beta testing (user validation) occur in Testing and Refinement. A/B testing occurs at or near Production Ramp-Up. Testing evolves across the pipeline from qualitative (should we build it?) through quantitative (how do we optimize it at scale?).

questions.question: What is the key difference between alpha testing and beta testing?

options.text: Alpha testing uses quantitative methods; beta testing uses qualitative methods
Alpha testing is conducted internally by company personnel; beta testing involves real external users testing in realistic conditions
Alpha testing occurs before concept generation; beta testing occurs after launch
Alpha testing measures awareness; beta testing measures purchase intention

questions.explanation: Alpha testing (performance and user interface testing) is conducted internally by company personnel to identify functional failures. Beta testing (validation/acceptance testing) involves real external users in realistic conditions to confirm the product meets user expectations at scale. Both occur during the Testing and Refinement phase, with alpha preceding beta. Neither is defined by the measurement method (qualitative vs. quantitative) but by who conducts the test.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct sequence of testing modalities in the product development process, from earliest to latest?

options.text: Alpha testing → Beta testing → Quantitative market testing → A/B testing
Qualitative concept testing → Specification testing → Alpha testing → A/B testing
Specification testing (benchmarking) → Prototype testing → Qualitative concept testing → Alpha testing → Quantitative market testing → Beta testing → A/B testing
Brainstorming → A/B testing → Quantitative market testing → Prototype testing

questions.explanation: The sequence follows the product development pipeline: Specification testing occurs during early design, qualitative concept testing in Concept Development, prototype testing and alpha/beta testing in Testing and Refinement, and A/B testing at release. Options (a) and (b) invert or omit key stages. Option (d) is nonsensical as brainstorming precedes all testing. Skipping any stage accelerates product failure risk.

questions.question: In the salsa conjoint analysis example, which attribute had the highest relative importance, and approximately what percentage did it represent?

options.text: Color (Red vs. Green) at approximately 60%
Thickness (Regular, Thick, Extra-Thick) at approximately 35%
Spiciness (Mild, Medium-Hot, Extra-Hot) at approximately 60%
Price at approximately 45%

questions.explanation: Spiciness had a relative importance of 59.8% (approximately 60%) — nearly six times the importance of Color (5.6%). Thickness was 34.6%. Price was not an attribute in the salsa study. This result tells the product manager to focus R&D investment on getting spiciness right (especially Mild), while color is essentially irrelevant for this consumer. Understanding importance weights guides product design prioritization.

questions.question: Using the hotel staycation conjoint results (OceanView coefficient = 5.83, HighPrice coefficient = −1.68, price increment = $50), what is the approximate willingness to pay for an Ocean view compared to a Garden view?

options.text: Approximately $42 more per night
Approximately $61 more per night
Approximately $174 more per night
Approximately $350 more per night

questions.explanation: WTP = (OceanView coefficient / |HighPrice coefficient|) × price increment = (5.83 / 1.68) × $50 = 3.47 × $50 ≈ $174. Option (a) corresponds to WTP for room size upgrade; option (b) to City view. The ocean view commands a $174 premium per night over garden view — by far the most valued attribute — meaning the hotel should price ocean-view rooms at least $174 above garden-view rooms.

questions.question: In the salsa conjoint regression (where rank 1 = most preferred), the coefficient on 'Thick' is −2.33. What does this negative coefficient indicate?

options.text: Thick salsa is less preferred than Regular salsa (ranked higher numerically)
Thick salsa is more preferred than Regular salsa (ranked lower numerically, closer to rank 1)
The coefficient is statistically insignificant at the 5% level
Thick and Regular salsa are equally preferred by the respondent

questions.explanation: Since rank 1 = most preferred, a lower rank number means better preference. A negative coefficient of −2.33 on Thick means that Thick salsa receives a rank 2.33 positions lower (better, closer to 1) than Regular salsa. In contrast, Extra-Thick has a positive coefficient (+3.83), meaning it receives a worse rank (further from 1) than Regular. The sign interpretation depends on the rank scale direction — a crucial detail students often confuse.

questions.question: How is the relative importance of an attribute calculated in conjoint analysis?

options.text: The average of all part-worth coefficients for that attribute, divided by the total number of attributes
(Attribute utility range / Sum of all attribute ranges) × 100, where range = max part-worth − min part-worth across all levels including baseline
The p-value of the largest coefficient in each attribute group
The absolute value of the largest coefficient, divided by the intercept

questions.explanation: Relative importance = (Range_j / ΣRange_k) × 100. For Thickness in the salsa example: range = 3.83 − (−2.33) = 6.16; for Spiciness: 10.67 − 0 = 10.67; for Color: 1.00 − 0 = 1.00. Total = 17.83. Thickness importance = 6.16/17.83 × 100 = 34.6%. Common mistake: forgetting to include the baseline (utility = 0) when computing the range — the full spread across all levels including the baseline must be used.

questions.question: Why are Randomized Controlled Trials (RCTs) considered the gold standard for establishing causality in marketing research?

options.text: They use very large sample sizes that guarantee statistical accuracy regardless of the research design
They rely on survey data, which is more reliable than observational behavioral data
Random assignment distributes confounding variables equally across groups in expectation, so only the treatment variable systematically differs between groups
They use regression analysis to perfectly control for all observable and unobservable confounders

questions.explanation: Random assignment is the key mechanism: it distributes all confounding variables (both observed and unobserved) equally across treatment and control groups in expectation. Any observed outcome difference can therefore be causally attributed to the treatment. Options (a) and (b) are incorrect: large samples reduce noise but do not guarantee causality; surveys are prone to stated-preference bias. Regression (d) controls only for observed confounders — unobserved confounders remain.

questions.question: In the St. John's Hospital Relocation project network, which path is the critical path and what is the minimum project duration?

options.text: Path A-I-K, duration 33 weeks
Path A-C-G-J-K, duration 67 weeks
Path B-D-H-J-K, duration 69 weeks
Path B-E-J-K, duration 43 weeks

questions.explanation: Path B-D-H-J-K has the longest duration: 9+10+40+4+6 = 69 weeks, making it the critical path. The minimum project duration is therefore 69 weeks. All other paths have slack: A-C-G-J-K has 2 weeks of slack; A-I-K has 36 weeks; B-E-J-K has 26 weeks; A-F-K has 41 weeks. Only activities on the critical path (B, D, H, J, K) have zero slack — any delay in them directly delays project completion.

questions.question: Activity B in the hospital relocation project has duration t = 9 weeks, ES = 0, and LF = 9 weeks. What is the activity's slack time (S)?

options.text: S = 9 weeks
S = 0 weeks (critical activity)
S = 18 weeks
S = 2 weeks

questions.explanation: LS = LF − t = 9 − 9 = 0. S = LS − ES = 0 − 0 = 0. Activity B is a critical activity with zero slack, confirming it lies on the critical path. Any delay to activity B delays the entire project by an equal amount. Option (d) — 2 weeks — is the slack for Activity A (not B), a common mistake when students confuse activities in the network.

questions.question: In the endowment effect experiment using Lindor chocolates, the regression model is: Value_i = a + b × Endowed_i. What does the coefficient b represent?

options.text: The average valuation reported by the control group (non-endowed)
The average valuation reported by the treatment group (endowed)
The estimated causal effect of ownership on valuation — the premium that owning the chocolate adds to willingness to pay
The correlation coefficient between ownership status and valuation

questions.explanation: In Y = a + bX with X = 1 if endowed: a = mean valuation of the control group (baseline); a + b = mean valuation of the treatment group; b = treatment effect = the causal increment in valuation caused by ownership (the endowment effect). Option (a) is the intercept 'a'; option (b) is a + b; option (d) confuses regression coefficients with correlation. Because assignment to treatment was random, b has a causal interpretation.

questions.question: Which of the following represents the correct sequence of the CBBE (Customer-Based Brand Equity) Pyramid from base to apex?

options.text: Resonance → Judgments/Feelings → Performance/Imagery → Salience
Salience → Performance/Imagery → Judgments/Feelings → Resonance
Performance → Salience → Resonance → Judgments/Feelings
Awareness → Image → Loyalty → Equity

questions.explanation: The CBBE pyramid progresses: Salience (Who are you?) → Performance/Imagery (What are you?) → Judgments/Feelings (What about you?) → Resonance (What about you and me?). Each layer must be established before the next can be built. You cannot achieve resonance without first establishing meaning (Performance/Imagery), and you cannot build meaning without first achieving salience. Option (a) inverts the pyramid; options (c) and (d) misrepresent the structure.

questions.question: Nike's brand mantra is 'Authentic Athletic Performance.' What is the general structure of a brand mantra according to Keller's framework?

options.text: Target customer + brand benefit + brand name
Emotional modifier + descriptive modifier + brand function
Points of parity + points of difference + brand promise
Brand awareness + brand image + brand positioning

questions.explanation: A brand mantra has three components: Emotional Modifier (characterizes the spirit — 'Authentic'), Descriptive Modifier (narrows the category — 'Athletic'), and Brand Function (the core value delivered — 'Performance'). Disney's mantra 'Fun Family Entertainment' follows the same structure. The mantra is internally facing — it guides decisions about products, campaigns, and distribution. It is not the tagline (externally facing) but the strategic compass.

questions.question: At the apex of the CBBE pyramid (Brand Resonance), which of the following correctly lists the four categories of resonance?

options.text: Quality, credibility, consideration, and superiority
Warmth, fun, excitement, and security
Behavioral loyalty, attitudinal attachment, sense of community, and active engagement
Performance, imagery, awareness, and recall

questions.explanation: Brand Resonance has four dimensions: behavioral loyalty (repeat purchase), attitudinal attachment (the brand feels special or loved), sense of community (affiliation with other users), and active engagement (the strongest form — consumers invest time/energy beyond purchase). Options (a) and (b) describe Brand Judgments and Brand Feelings (Level 3), not Resonance. Option (d) is not a level of the pyramid. Active engagement is particularly valuable because it drives organic advocacy and reduces marketing spend.

questions.question: The Won Soju case study features a premium repositioning strategy for a previously commoditized product. Which brand management concept does Jay Park's strategy of open-run pop-up queuing events and celebrity cultural capital most directly illustrate?

options.text: Brand dilution through category overextension
The Parmentier logic: right endorser + curated first experience + deliberate meaning reframing
Brand repositioning using a perceptual map to identify white space
Brand salience — building deep and broad awareness through mass media

questions.explanation: The Parmentier logic applies: (1) right endorser — Jay Park's K-hip-hop celebrity cultural capital transfers desirability associations; (2) curated first experience — pop-up stores with queuing events create aspirational, exclusive touchpoints rather than transactional retail; (3) deliberate meaning reframing — soju repositioned as a premium lifestyle spirit for millennials rather than a cheap commodity. This parallels Parmentier's 18th-century strategy of having Louis XVI wear potato blossoms to change category perception without functional claims.

questions.question: According to Bain & Company data cited in Lecture 8, what combined market share of the personal luxury goods market do Millennials and Gen Z consumers represent, projected for 2025?

options.text: Approximately 36%
Approximately 44%
Approximately 63%
Over 70%, accounting for 130% of market growth

questions.explanation: Bain data: combined Millennial + Gen Z share was 44% in 2019 (36%+8%), rose to 63% in 2021 (46%+17%), and is projected to exceed 70% by 2025, accounting for 130% of market growth. This massive generational shift is the central structural force reshaping luxury brand strategy — forcing brands to balance New Luxury (experiential, digital, culturally engaged) with heritage equity preservation.

questions.question: What is the primary strategic difference between disruptive repositioning and incremental repositioning in brand management?

options.text: Disruptive repositioning always increases brand equity; incremental repositioning always reduces it
Disruptive repositioning makes a rapid, pronounced break from established brand identity; incremental repositioning gradually evolves positioning while maintaining continuity
Disruptive repositioning is used only for fashion brands; incremental repositioning is for mass-market brands
Disruptive repositioning enters new product categories; incremental repositioning improves existing products

questions.explanation: Disruptive repositioning, as executed by Alessandro Michele at Gucci, involves a sharp movement on the perceptual map — moving from Classic/older-skewing to Contemporary/younger-skewing — breaking from established identity. Incremental repositioning gradually shifts perception while maintaining continuity with existing equity. Disruptive repositioning carries higher risk (alienating existing customers, dilution risk) but also higher potential reward (capturing new segments). The perceptual map provides the visualization tool for diagnosing the repositioning challenge.

questions.question: What is brand dilution, and what is the fundamental strategic tension it creates for luxury brands?
questions.correctAnswer: Brand dilution is the weakening of a brand's distinctive associations, perceived exclusivity, and perceived quality — typically caused by overextension into inappropriate categories, excessive licensing, or overly broad distribution. For luxury brands, the core tension is between accessibility (extending into lower-price-point categories like beauty to grow revenue and reach) and exclusivity (maintaining the scarcity and aspirational distance that justify premium pricing and underpin the brand's signaling value). More accessible products can undermine the luxury brand's core equity even while generating short-term revenue.

questions.question: Describe the Three D's framework for personal brand positioning. For each D, name the dimension, explain what it tests, and identify the corresponding dimension in the classic product innovation challenge Venn diagram.
questions.correctAnswer: The Three D's are: (1) Desirability — tests whether others genuinely want what you stand for and whether your positioning reflects genuine personal passion; maps to 'People/Desirable' in the innovation challenge. (2) Deliverability — tests whether you have the skills, track record, and capability to consistently and credibly deliver on your positioning; maps to 'Technical/Feasible.' (3) Differentiation — tests whether your positioning is sufficiently distinctive and sustainable long-term that it carves out a unique market position others cannot easily replicate; maps to 'Business/Viable.' The optimal personal brand sits at the intersection of all three, paralleling the innovation sweet spot where desirability, feasibility, and viability align simultaneously.
